speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This fifth conversation with Arthur Sundberg takes place at his home in Potlatch on August 7, 1975.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Corliss Engine. I've heard that that Corliss Engine was really a monster. Yeah, they had two of them. They had one that drove the sawmill and then they had one that drove the cleaning mill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Of course, the one that drove the sawmill was half again larger than the one that drove the cleaner. But the one that drove the sawmill, they kept it in place down there after it was taken out of service, oh, for a few years. And, oh, there was a lot of different discussions as to what to do with it. The old Jew scrap iron dealer, he wanted it for scrap because it was the highest quality scrap that you could possibly find. But he wanted to buy it for so much a ton on board a car. Well, management approached me on it and I discouraged them on that because they wouldn't only give the engine away, but they'd lose money too because it would take, it would cost more money to take it out of there than it would to what they'd get for it for scrap. So they just sat there. Well then finally, a man by the name of Williams up here in Spokane, he's a brother to this Williams that makes his time and temperature signs, you know, you've seen these electric signs that change every minute and gives you the temperatures.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: He talked him into giving it to him, but he would take it out, that is, he would pay for taking it out. And he was going to put it up in Spokane, it's out there by Medical Lake, along with a lot of other old sawmill mining and farm equipment and make a museum, you know, for the public. So they give it to him and

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: so he approached me, wanted me to take it out. That was after I'd retired and I told him no way would I take it out because I said I don't do any lifting or slugging or any of that heavy work. Well he said he didn't want me to do any work, he said he'd get iron workers from Spokane do the work, but he wanted me to supervise it, you know, to tell him how to take it apart and then mark all the pieces so they could be reassembled. So I said I agreed to that, so I supervised the taking the engine down and shipping it to Spokane. But, oh that thing, it run from the time the mill started and I'm not entirely certain whether that was a brand new engine when it was installed here. You see that was built by this Minneapolis company and there was only,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: seems to me like there was only three engine that was ever built of that size and the other two I believe was installed in some big breweries back in Wisconsin I think. Well there was one in Minneapolis and I think there was one in Wisconsin. But at the time that that was dismantled they had to, or they disconnected the big steam pipe. There was a 12 inch steam line that supplied the engine, but they disconnected that and just hooked up to a two inch airline to it and there wasn't any load on it. You know, so periodically when they had visitors coming through, a bunch of visitors, then they'd start up the engine and let them see it run. It would just run on air pressure you see, but no load. But that thing run it like a dual watch.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well a person really should have a picture of it to see the, to get some idea of the size of it you know. The flywheel on it was

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: 60 inches across and 24 feet in diameter. And the spokes on that wheel you know, the spokes were about like so you know. Of course they weren't round, they were you know kind of flattened. It was all cast iron. The frame was all cast iron and the only thing that was steel was the connecting rod and of course the bearings were bronze bearings with the inserts. But they had a 48 inch cylinder, 48 inches in diameter cylinder, just one cylinder. And

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: get the stroke on it. But anyway, that was

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: 1200 horsepower in one engine. That was quite a piece of machinery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you explain briefly how it worked, the Corliss Engine?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well it's a little bit difficult to explain unless a person is acquainted with you know this

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I could I could make you just a small sketch of the operating mechanism. Anyway there was eccentrics on the main crankshaft between the the main bearing and the flywheel. There was two big eccentrics on the on the shaft there. Now if you know what an eccentric is, that's something that runs off -center you see. Well then connected to these eccentrics was push rods, steel push rods. These rods are about 20 feet long and they run back to the steam chest. And of course they worked on the outside of the steam chest. On the steam chest end of these push rods.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They worked an eccentric there of course on the steam chest. This eccentric just rocked back and forth like so you see. One at each end of this cylinder. And then there was a rods that come down from that down and they opened and closed valves. Now they had rotary valves that went through. There was an intake and an exhaust valve on each end of this cylinder. And these rods would open and close these rotary valves you see. So that when the intake valve was open on one end the exhaust valve had to be open on the opposite end and then vice versa you see. And that pushed this cylinder piston back and forth in the cylinders.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was the steam generated?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well from the power plant they had battery boilers there. They had 12

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: return flue boilers. And then they had one of course this big water tube boiler wasn't installed until 1920 beginning in 1925.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But anyway they they burned all their waste from the mill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Not all of it but they burned whatever waste they needed from the mill to generate the steam. And then the surplus waste went into a big burner. This burner was

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: oh I don't know the exact dimension was about 60 feet in diameter and I think it was 110 feet tall with a screen top on it with all the surplus slabs and sawdust and stuff that they didn't need that went into this burner.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: That burner it was torn down.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh it was in the 1940s I can't give you the exact year. But anyway they quit burning their surplus. And then they hogged all the fuel that is all the slabs went through a big hog knife hog that chopped it all up into small pieces you know. And then whatever wasn't burned in the power plant they put in a conveyor system that went across to a big fuel pile where they could store this fuel where they could bring it back in and burn it on weekends holidays and times whenever the mill wasn't running you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the the Corliss engine provided the power for the entire sawmill?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Everything. Everything. Entire sawmill plus the green chain.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: The result driven I think I told you here before about the belt that used on that engine that went from the engine over into the sawmill.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: They claimed that the it took 3 ,000 cow hides to make that one belt.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Of course now when you see a cow hide in making a belt they don't use the entire hide because the flank and some of those parts of the hide are too soft you see they just use the backs. And they used mostly South American cow hides because they said that they were thicker than they I suppose it was done in a warm climate you know. But this thing's all put together with glue and

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: whenever whenever this belt would stretch you know which it would you know after put on a new belt well then it would run about a year away then it'd stretch to the point where they'd have to take a piece out of it and re -glue it you know. It takes an experienced man to do that kind of work.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Each layer in thickness of this belt has to be separated back for a distance of about three feet on each end of the belt you know where it's cut. And then each ply has to be separated back and then these plies have to be shaved down to a feather edge you see. And then they have to be covered with glue and then these plies would go together like so and then they had great big wooden clamps that they they clamped on that and they'd stay in these clamps until that glue was dry.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And to make that joint so that it smooths so that it wouldn't pound as it went over the pulley you know. You take on the driven end the small pulley why it could make it quite a racket you know and it could it could put a lot of strain on the pulley itself and on the bearings on the shaft you know if it if it had found every time it come over and of course the speed that it go over. It would be just like a hammer striking you know if it wasn't good and smooth so it was it was skilled work you know putting a belt like that together. Well then it's kind of hard to someone to picture

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: the transmission of power in the mill like that. Unless you could look at some drawings or sketches or something you get some idea. Now then this shaft supported the main line shaft in the sawmill where the engine drove on to this main line shaft it was seven inches in diameter and and then of course it oh it was probably 60 feet long but on this shaft were several other pulleys and that transmitted the power to other shafts big long shafts and they were these other shafts would transmit the power to the main bandsaws you know they had four bandsaws so they they had two of them would would drive off in one shaft well then from that secondary shaft it was it would drive over on to a third shaft and of course each one would it had progressed over would be a little smaller in size but anyway that that mill was 120 feet wide and about 340 feet long when you stand on the floor and look up like that it was just a maze of shafts and pulleys and belts over the entire area.

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well then you take say that the main main shaft run this way well then there was a another line shaft that run diagonally to that to 90 degrees to that that drove the edges well to get this power off in this shaft to drive a shaft is running 90 degrees to that that had to be done through some gears and those gears were they were called core gears and and these these gears were about five feet in diameter and they were a cast iron hub with slots in it and these slots they put in wooden teeth and these teeth had to be fitted in shape so that they went in there and then a key would be driven in to tighten that tooth into the into the casting. Well then you see you had bevel gear running in this plane another bevel gear running in this plane and of course they match perfectly together so that you transmit power in one direction to the other. Well then to drive the the green chain there was a from this main shaft there was another belt that go over onto another counter shaft and then of course it run in the same plane as this main shaft but then the the power that went out to the green chain it had to run right angles to that so there had to be another big core gear on that one you see that would transmit the power out to the and it's really a stupendous piece of engineering you take you take the engineering here today you can set a machine down in any location and at any angle it doesn't make any difference how a machine is set down on its base because you would detach the electric motor right to it and that had but when you when you're going to run a sawmill as big as this one was it in the earlier years and run it off in just one power source that that was really a piece of engineering that's something that

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: future generations will have no idea whatsoever of what it was and it's too bad that I thought lots of times it's there

some money well you take if you just if you just stop and figure now say a man gets a job and his income for the month would be say seventy dollars that's his total income for the month

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: but that seventy dollars he has to pay a few dollars for rent he has to buy his groceries by clothing and in some cases he would probably make a small payment on whatever furniture he had to buy each month

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and then of course in most cases why they would probably use a little bit of money for recreation might want to go to the baseball game

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and then another thing too is that the I think the majority of the families at that period of time by the man of the house he probably drank a lot of booze he spent quite a little bit of money for booze and I know that the people that I knew and was in contact with at the time that I was growing up that's very much held very much true and it was generally the woman in the house that really had to manage to keep food on the table and keep her kids clothed and ready for school and all that sort of thing because the man would work hard and he would come home and he'd eat and pile in the bed and next morning he'd get up and go to work but then the weekend would come and he'd go out and he'd get drunk on Saturday night and he'd stay drunk on Monday morning so there was really no money to be saved now there was a big percentage of the women that had families, the husband was working but still the women would either take in boarders or do laundry for single men and do all kinds of things they'd probably go out and do some housework for some of the brass in order to get a little additional income so that they could buy a pair of shoes for Johnny or a dress for Nellie or something like that but as far as saving money was concerned that was just almost the thing that you couldn't even hope for and that's the way the country was built that's the way conditions were I think from the time that the country was started up until about when we got straightened out after the depression period in the 30s so that was a long period of time that the country was run just that way you take people your age and most of the young people around the country they've got no conception at all of how people used to live I think after even being told and even if you've seen pictures and you could read books and stuff it's still hard to really imagine and picture in your mind just the way people lived and I wouldn't say that they were suffering any hardships because that was just the way people lived that was their mode of living and they at the time probably didn't think that they was being put through any particular hardship

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: now you take for instance even people that were fairly well to do they still had to go out in the backyard to that little house out there to go to the john

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and in the winter time you'd go out there you'd wrap up with mufflers and sweaters and wrap up mittens and everything to go out to the john but they didn't seem to mind it because that's all they knew they didn't know anything else now if you go to the bathroom you've got to have it's got to be heated it's got to be heated with a furnace or electric heater or some other method of heating it and people that's working under the most menial jobs right now live better than the very top did years ago

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: you know I can recall when I was a little kid there was three of us kids in the family I have one brother and one sister and you know in order to provide some real what my mother considered something that we really needed in our diet she probably had two eggs and she would boil those two eggs and probably mash them up and then divide it up into three parts we each had a part of an egg

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and we didn't think that we was really being deprived but we just had to do the women folks especially they had their heads had to be going all the time to figure out how to keep things going because that's something that the men didn't take any part in at all you might say they went out and they worked and they brought in part of their paycheck at least and it was up to the woman to see that everything was held together you mentioned to me before that these people on Knob Hill were many of them living beyond their means well that's very true you take all that spokesman review newspaper daily you know daily and Sunday and the kid would peddle that house every day and in the winter time he'd have to break his own trail they wouldn't even shovel the sidewalks and get up there to the house with that newspaper and that newspaper cost 70 cents a month and that kid probably in some of those houses up there he'd probably make five or six trips up there trying to get the 70 cents and they'd keep stalling him off and stalling him off and stalling him off they're short of change we're just a little bit short you'd have to come back and have more darn excuses by golly to keep them paying that kid for that paper but you take the average person in this part of the town over here the kid would come and get their money for the paper they'd give him his money right there but up there on that hill they didn't what's the cause of that being I mean if they didn't were they making just about what they had to be making more than the average worker I know it but then generally their wives had more parties and probably would buy a little more expensive clothes or something that used it up but in a way it was simply jaw bone the whole thing it was just putting on a front and they didn't have anything there was a few of them up there you know that were responsible that you know they weren't all that way but there was a few up there that all they wanted to do was be able to get a vacant house where they could get up on that hill up there and when they got up there of course they paid more rent when they got up there too you know but yeah to begin with but then of course I say management and the of course the doctor he was that was private enterprise and

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and but to begin with why it was built in a in a square four four sided square and they got a the square still open up there you if you've been up there while you see it well there's three houses on the west side of the square and there's three houses on the east side of the square and then there was two houses on the north well there were three houses on the north side of the square and only one on the south side so it was just in that in that square and of course the

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: general manager the assistant general manager and and then the man that was the assistant that was in charge of all lands you know that's timber and town and everything else then there was a master mechanic that is he was the master mechanic of the railroad shops and the machine shops you know that is they had a roundhouse here and they had five locomotives in there and they had these passenger coaches do they run a passenger train to maintain so the master mechanic he lived in one of them and then of course they had the sales manager and assistant sales manager and then they had the store manager and they had the planning mill superintendent and then they had the preacher preacher he was up there too but that was the beginning the population around the square you see well at that time the population of that square I don't believe you'd find any of those deadbeats in there at that time you know but then they start building a few more houses on the square and they get a little more population well then then when you start running into deadbeats

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever heard it called snob hill?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Oh I think so I heard it called that not too long ago but just the common name for it was bug hill everybody called it bug hill see and I suppose that's because they figured that was where the big bugs were you know that's an expression that used to be used but of course they liked to have it called knob hill but then bug hill was just the ordinary expression for that particular area

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you some about safety and accidents in the mill I was curious about the you know we talked about how it was open and there really were no safety devices in the early days How much awareness was there among the men you know I mean how careful how was there an idea where you got to watch what you do carefully?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You might have what you might call total awareness because they knew that they was working in those areas where they had all these hazards But I think as far as my memory goes that after they started guarding machines and putting in safer walk areas and all that thing they had more accidents after they put in guards than they ever had before they put in guards

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: because when a machine is guarded people just tend to be more relaxed and careless of course whether a machine is guarded or not guarded they still have some accidents on account of loose clothing and stuff like that you know a person can get wound up

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: but if you'd see some of the machines that they used to run and the size the immensity of it you know is scary to death until you got used to that area

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: but I'll just give you an example of one thing that they used some and if you had a conveyor that was conveying sawdust and slabs and then it was emptying into either another conveyor going in the same direction or going at right angles to that out at the end of this conveyor they would have some spike rolls now they would probably have rolls that were probably 8 inches in diameter and they were drilled and tapped at about maybe 3 inch spacings length of the roll and then each roll was 3 inches apart?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: no, each roll of these holes that they drilled in there and then they would tap those out with threading you see well then they would take square headed set screws and the blacksmiths would heat those and point those up to where these heads come up to a sharp point you know four sided and they would screw them in to these holes so you see on one roll there would be probably a hundred of these spikes and there would be about two of these rolls there at the end of this conveyor well the sawdust would fall through between these rolls but the slabs these spikes would take them and throw them over into this other conveyor you see well now you can imagine what would happen if your clothes come in contact with a thing like that you know just before like this you see with those sharp real sharp spikes in there it could really wind you up couldn't it see but men knew what that was and they gave it plenty of clearance and I don't recall anyone getting wound up in when they spike rolls and as far as fatalities are concerned over the years, you take in the earlier days there was one man killed unloading logs at the log pond they come in on these railroad cars and they had a street loaded cars on one track and then right next to it was another track where a steam crane travelled and they had these cables that was under the load and the man on the ground would hook these cables from the crane onto these cables that was under the load and then they would take up the slack to hold it up you know then he would break this vid hook on this chain on the binder chains you see to release the load and then the crane would dump the load into the pond you see well one day there was a log on top that as they started to lift that log swung and it came off the wrong way and it hit this man and killed him

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and I believe in what you might consider the earlier period here that was about the only fatality that I can recall but then in more modern times after they got a lot of guards and areas protected and signs, safety signs all over the place and safety meetings where they would discuss safety and all kinds of safety inspections then they started killing people there wasn't too many but there was a few there was one man who was working on the construction crew down there he came out of a dry sorter shed well in there while this lumber had come out of the dry kiln it would come out on a chain and they would pull it off into different bins and into piles and then they had overhead crane traveling crane that traveled on the monorail and they'd pick up these loads and pick them up and carry them out outside the building and then set them down on bunks where one of these straddle carriers would come up and get it and haul it away you see well this particular day while this man was in this building and he came out at the end of the building right out under this roadway and of course that was all concrete paved and thinking about something else and walked right out in front of one of them straddle carriers and knocked him down and a wheel ran over his head and his head busted open like a dropped watermelon

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: and another time right in the same area there was an electrician's helper that was up on the roof of the building and there was a high power line crossing there and so he thought that this juice wouldn't jump as far as it did so he didn't duck very far going underneath this line and it knocked him off and of course he fell about 30 feet down onto the ground and killed him and there was another time where there was an oiler in the sawmill and he was oiling some of the transmission shafting out on the green chain and this particular drive was all boxed in boxed in solid all the way around so there was a door on one side where he could open it up and turn down these grease cups on this side and he was supposed to walk around the other side and open a little door there and turn down the grease cups on the other side but this particular day he didn't do that he reached through there and when he did he went in between the belt and the pulley and it just crushed his head

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: so that's the way it goes when it, now I don't advocate having open machinery running, you know everything should be guarded but still in all when it is guarded it's carelessness of people that is the reason mostly for them getting hurt or getting killed

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: there's a few mechanical failures that are responsible but most of them are human failures

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any jobs in the early days that were regarded as dangerous more than others?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well I think you might say that they were all dangerous as far as that goes because wherever you had any moving machinery there's always a hazard

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: but gosh people grew up with it and they'd probably been around it all their life and you take for a stranger going into one of those areas even if I could, if we could go back to when they had run this big engine and they had all that belt transmission of power throughout the whole mill and if I could take you down there today and take you on a tour around there you'd be ducking and squirming and you'd be scared to death before you even got started

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take just for instance static electricity and some of those belts there you could put your hand up like that and the fire would fly out of all your fingers there a distance of 3 or 4 inches, a blue flame would just fly and if you walked by there your hair would just stand right up just like that you know and that was always one thing that usually always was shown to visitors They used to have quite a few visitors at night time you know, people that'd be working days you know, women and men too in someplace else they'd like to go down and visit the mill at night in the early part of the evening you know and then you'd, of course it wasn't compulsory that you have a guide you know, anybody could come and go as they pleased and they didn't question anybody but usually though if somebody come down there you know in the evening you know, probably some men and women or maybe just some women there'd always be some of us around there that you know would volunteer to act as a guide and take them around and of course it was always, you never miss that thing, I think possibly you remember reading this old lady you know she was telling about electricity and the belt of static electricity but anyway that was, just about everybody that come in there you know as a visitor they'd always take them around there where they could hold their hand up there and see the fire fly out of their fingers

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were accidents fairly common?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I don't mean critical ones but just injuries on the job No, I wouldn't say that they were, I think for the number of man hours worked and all that I think they were held down to a very reasonable percentage

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, you take in any walk of life, any work,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: some guy is bound to drop a crate or drop a board and you know mash up his toe or mash his finger and some people are more prone to accidents than other people I think you've probably seen people too that stumble over their own feet falling down continuously, people like that are dangerous and you can't let people like that work in an area where there is a hazard

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I think that in most cases where men proved to be accident prone, well then they probably tried to move him into some other area where he probably wouldn't get hurt They were pretty considerate about all those things, that's one thing that regardless of the time or the age, people don't like to see somebody else get hurt

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember any injuries that were really lucky not to have been fatal?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Yeah, I remember one man that, they were building a tower to support a blowpipe that is the big pipe that carried shavings And this tower was up possibly around 40 feet in the air and he fell off in that tower and you know by rights you might say he might have gotten killed But he busted his leg and he laid in the hospital for a long time and I went in to see him And that's the first time I ever seen a contraption like he was in

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gosh, when I seen that thing going through his knee there, I said, God, don't that thing hurt? No, he said he didn't feel at all And I said, well, what about taking that out of there?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Well, he said, that pin is tapered a little bit and he said he asked the doctor about that and the doctor said, oh, he just, they can tap it on one end and it'd pop right out of there and I guess it did But that was one accident that,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: oh, it probably could have been more but it's a little hard to recall all of them things, you know I'm just saying that guys really thought about, really did think about it a lot in the early days, I mean they had to think about the safety and work and were just naturally very careful about what they did Well, they were very careful and to prove that they were careful was that they were still there, you know And of course, I don't think they really had to be told to be careful because everything was so open and so frightening all around them that anybody that really wanted to live, they had to take it on themselves to be careful and watch what they were doing

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that in a situation like that, men would get superstitious, I mean, you know, just have real ways of combating the risks?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, I don't believe that superstition had anything to do with it It was just a man that said, trained himself for that particular job Now, whether or not he was an oiler or whether he was a millwright, if he was an oiler he had to understand something about how much temperature a bearing might stand, you know If it got too hot he had to have some knowledge as to how to combat that heat until the time that the mill would go down

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And if he was a millwright he had to be a mechanic, so he was a mechanic working in this particular area But the millwright's work was usually done when the mill was idle, that is, you couldn't do any repair work while it was running except maybe some clobbered up makeshift thing to keep it running until time to shut down And of course there was a good many times when, you know, over the years the mill had to shut down during the running period on account of a breakdown or something Well, whenever that happened, why the superintendent, he was over there just right now, it just wouldn't be only a few minutes until he'd be on the scene He wanted an estimate right away as to how long it was going to take to make that repair and get it running again Well, say that something broke down at 10 .30 and it was going to take them an hour or probably an hour and a half to fix it They wouldn't let the crew stand around there, they'd just send them home, come back at noon

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: And that's the way those things were handled, you see, but they, generally the superintendent, he wanted an estimate right away as to how long it was going to take to get it fixed And if the millwright foreman was wrong on his estimation, well then he generally got chewed out pretty good, you know, because if they called the men back and the thing wasn't ready yet to run Well then they would have to pay these men wages until it did get ready to run, see, and they didn't want to do that, they didn't want to pay anybody that wasn't working That sounds like a pretty tough responsibility for the millwright foreman because I would think it would be difficult to know in some cases just what it would do Well I know but then if a man, if a man had worked, if that worked long enough and had trained well enough to be the foreman, then he should have a pretty darn good idea of what it would take Now you're working in big heavy machinery, but still in all you've got a good idea of what it would take because you've probably done something very similar to that before And of course sometimes you'll run into some difficulty that your estimate will go haywire And I know one time in particular there was a

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: Coupling that, well,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: I was telling you about these big core gears, these big gears with the wooden teeth in them Well, there's been several times that those things would strip all the teeth right out of them, you know, if you allow them to wear to where you're getting slack in there Well if one tooth would break, well then of course it might just break the whole thing So they stripped the core gear one day and they generally had another one all made up with the teeth already in it and all they had to do was just set the keys Of course there was a key between every two teeth, you see, it was just the shape of this wood that, when they'd drive this key in and pull it tight into the socket Well, in order to, the quickest thing to do would be to take that core gear off and put the other one on because the other one with the teeth was already fitted to it, see But otherwise they would have to fit a new set of teeth to this core and that would require a lot more time So they was going to change this core gear, well in order to do that they had to take a coupling off in the shaft, this coupling, you know, so big around, you know, on a seven inch shaft

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they had to jack it up and then take one half of the coupling off, you know, off in the part of the shaft where the core gear was And then get the key out of this core gear and slide it off the shaft and put the other one on Well, now I've got it, you know, some way or other when they put it back together they had the core gear on and they had it back in place And they was driving the key into this coupling, you know, keying the coupling to the shaft And I don't know just who it was that had done it or how it was done, but anyway, they drove the key in from the wrong side Of course this keyway is tapered, you see, and so they bolted it all together again and started up when they did by this half of the coupling busted And that super identity was just fit to be tied because, you know, he had an estimate of how long it was going to take to do this job And then of course they had to do this all over again and rustle up another half of coupling and put on there and, you know, a lot of machine work and everything else Boy, he was just like a crazy man around there But, you know, that's probably one incident in a good many years, you know, but it's just something like that, you know, that can happen I was wondering, too, about this longer, the long -term effect, I mean, we talked about, you mentioned the deafness, and I'm thinking now of accidents, hazards But I'm thinking of, you know, what happens when you're doing hard work for decades, some guys working in the mill And I'm wondering what other kinds of job disabilities you could develop besides deafness I've heard one thing, that riding the saws could be really hard on a man to do it for years You mean riding the carriage, the rig?

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: No, I don't think so Usually the men that were chosen to do that work were men that were real light and catty on their feet, you know And somebody that has to have a good sense of balance And although you take the sawmill noise, the noise of the saws and the gears and roll cases and everything rolling and everything It has a lower pitch sound that really, I don't think, affects a person's ability to hear quite as much as in the planing mill Now in the planing mill,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: say that you got,

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: in the old days there was 22 machines in the planer And they were all just about pitched up to about the same sound pitch, you see Just a real high pitched sound, and boy you could really lose your hearing there I was never, never worked in there long enough to ever affect me, but then I know men that worked there, you know, made a lifetime of it Why most all of them were almost totally deaf

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: But you take nowadays, with all their safety engineers and their highly educated people Any area of any noise area at all, even though it's very low, they have to wear ear protectors, ear muffs, you know, the safety gear Are there any other disabilities that you can think of that would develop through years of, I know in the woods and in some of the small sawmills That a lot of guys have developed back problems and things like that from the work that they did for a long time Well that can happen, I think, in most any category of work

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You know, some men are qualified to do a job and other men are not, even though they work at it A man might work at a job for years and years and still never be qualified for that work, because he just can't adapt himself to that kind of work You take in the case of lifting something, well you know, one man can lift a certain load and another man can kick it back One man knows how to do it and another man doesn't know how to do it, and that's what you run into And I think the smaller the operation, the more of this awkwardness you'll find Because they've never been trained either by seeing somebody else do it or doing it themselves in a more efficient way And I think the bigger the operation, the more efficient people are in their methods of doing things

speaker: Arthur Sundberg
words: You take in a little one -horse operation out here in the woods, somebody starts up a little sawmill Well, they got probably a circle saw for sawing and for loading the log onto the carriage and for turning the log He has to use a PV or a can hook to roll it over by hand And then of course the tail saw here, the board comes off the saw and he has to push it down this rollway by hand possibly to get it out of the way So you see, the smaller the operation, the less efficient it is And it seems like they show the least skill It isn't that they are least skilled, but it's probably required
